ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai papaya salad or Papaya Paradise Salad. FOODON_03311737 (papaya food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific salad-dish term is not available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents raw papaya as a commodity. No composite Thai papaya salad dish code exists in FoodEx2. Used as closest parent anchor for the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Thai papaya salad or Papaya Paradise Salad as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of composite Southeast Asian salad dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Papaya Paradise Salad is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one restaurant plate as typically served in Thai restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Som Tam (Green Papaya Salad) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Papaya Paradise Salad' exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for raw green papaya (FDC ID 169926), cherry tomatoes, long beans, lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar, dried shrimp, chilli, garlic, and roasted peanuts, weighted by typical Thai green papaya salad recipe proportions. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table entries for Som Tam. The restaurant name 'Papaya Paradise Salad' at The Upper House – Funan is a branded menu name for a dish consistent with Thai green papaya salad (Som Tum). Nutrient values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the prepared salad.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for green papaya salad / raw papaya
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Papaya Paradise Salad' as a branded dish. GI of 38 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw papaya (GI ~58–60) and the overall low-carbohydrate, high-fibre, high-acid (lime juice) composition of Thai green papaya salad, which substantially lowers the effective GI relative to ripe papaya alone. The composite dish GI is estimated at 38 (low), consistent with published analogous estimates for Som Tum in the peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2021). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (38 × 9.2 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 7.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g serving of Papaya Paradise Salad provides approximately 840 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce and dried shrimp, which represents about 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or substituting with low-sodium soy sauce can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 7 per serving, Papaya Paradise Salad is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high dietary fibre content (approximately 4.2 g per serving) and acidic lime dressing further attenuate postprandial glucose response, though palm sugar content should be monitored in strict carbohydrate-controlled diets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vitamin C and antioxidants, but elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Papaya Paradise Salad is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.8 g per serving) and provides beneficial antioxidants including vitamin C (approximately 76 mg per serving) and beta-carotene from papaya, supporting cardiovascular health; however, the elevated sodium content from fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and a reduced-sodium preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Papaya Paradise Salad is a Thai-inspired fresh salad dish served at The Upper House – Funan in Singapore, based on the classic Thai green papaya salad known as Som Tum (ส้มตำ). The dish is prepared by shredding unripe green papaya into fine julienne strips and combining it with cherry tomatoes, long beans, dried shrimp, roasted peanuts, fresh chilli, and garlic, dressed with a tangy-sweet-savoury sauce made from lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar. The resulting dish has a crisp, crunchy texture from the raw papaya and long beans, a bright acidic flavour from the lime, a savoury umami depth from the fish sauce and dried shrimp, and a gentle heat from the chilli. Nutritionally, the salad is low in calories (approximately 136 kcal per 200 g serving), low in fat, and provides a meaningful amount of dietary fibre, vitamin C, and vitamin A from the papaya. The glycemic index is estimated at 38 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is the sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp, which can be significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish and shellfish allergens from the fish sauce and dried shrimp, and peanut allergens from the roasted peanuts. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard preparation. In Singapore's Thai restaurant scene, this dish is commonly ordered as a starter or light main course and is valued for its refreshing, low-calorie profile.

Is green papaya salad good for weight loss?
Can I eat Papaya Paradise Salad if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 'Papaya Paradise Salad' exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw ingredient data and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table entries for Som Tum, weighted by standard Thai green papaya salad recipe proportions. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the dish is a recognisable Thai green papaya salad variant and the ingredient composition is well-established, but the specific restaurant preparation at The Upper House – Funan may vary in dressing ratios and portion size.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw ingredient data (green papaya FDC ID 169926, cherry tomatoes, long beans, fish sauce, palm sugar, dried shrimp, chilli, garlic, roasted peanuts) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Som Tum, weighted by standard recipe proportions. No exact approved-source entry for this branded dish exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish or its primary ingredients. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA composite for this dish type. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats or ruminant animal fats in meaningful quantities — confirmed zero per USDA ingredient data. 5. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this branded dish exists. 6. Halal status is marked false because the dish contains fish sauce and dried shrimp from unverified halal-certified sources; the dish may be halal if certified ingredients are used, but this cannot be confirmed from available data. 7. The cuisine_tags 'BBQ' and 'Fried Rice Vermicelli' in the input record appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags not relevant to this dish and have been disregarded. 8. FoodOn match type is closest_parent; FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent; SNOMED CT match type is none — all documented above.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA for Som Tum composite. Added Thai local name as required by G6. Added Chinese and Malay names for Singapore context. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 with zero justification in notes. Set GI to 38 (analogous estimate, low) with full gi_source documentation. Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. All hard gates pass; score 7/8 (q8 failed).
